author: @tonyb83
text: Great explanation from 2:05 to 2:35 about keeping things simple. Why do car manufacturers make internal combustion engine cars with so much complicated electronics? What are the ALL the costs and benefits of doing it? By 'ALL' I  mean every cost and benefit.... to the manufacturer, the customer (who can't maintain the car as easily and as chealpy as he or she could years ago with a Haynes manual), society as a whole, the environment (pollution, raw materials etc and the fact that cars don't last as long as they used to (especially with the help of a Haynes manual) and so on and on and on....ALL the costs and benefits...

author: @NBC_NCO
text:
Greetings from the USA.

 Complexity with modern vehicles. Vs 6os and 70s vehicle's .

As a mechanic I really hate most modern vehicle's because of their Complexity.  I cringe every time a see a European vehicle pulls into my shop. The worst of the modern cars in my opinion. 

Even a simple oil change takes 2 X longer  with all the plastic panels. 

These owners of say Mercedes, complain about the repair cost but want to pretend their poo smells like Roses. 

A bit of a rant..but your right...the more sophisticated the machine,  the more expensive and time consuming the repair. 

I love the 60s and 70s American cars. So simple. Lots of room. Very seldom busting knuckles.

author: @heathcliffebird7514
text: Hi. Quick question… if i wanted to heat a space but wanted to keep the combustion outside, would it be possible to build something like a rocket stove for maximum energy extraction and then duct air around it through pipes on the chimney and use convection to draw air through… then just put the air in and out ducts indoors and put the fire some place safer without having to worry about carbon monoxide, smoke, etc? :-) i live in an old 60s council block that used to use convention driven airflow and an external furnace for communal heating. Wondering if that would work safely on a smaller scale. loving the channel btw. Been binge watching for a few days straight. Thanks for doing this

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah - you could isolate the two with a heat pump

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
I've been thinking about this very same concept.  My solution is to have two steel barrels/drums of different size--smaller one inside the larger one. The smaller one and the horizontal firebox will contain the fire (I will line it with something so that the steel doesn't degrade), but the larger barrel will be isolated from the direct fire and smoke, and there will be a pipe going from the space in between the barrels vented into a window (there will be a fiberglass reinforced foam insert in the window with a vent hole cut in it).  The larger barrel will have to have a small, cool air intake hole somewhere so that undue pressure doesn't build up. 

    The tricky part will be sealing the horizontal fire box space between the two barrels (i.e. the lower part of the "L").  For the inner barrel, I can just cut a little "doorway" completely out, but I think for the larger barrel--I would want to cut it strategically and bend it back into the other barrel and then seal up the spaces with something like cob.  Because you really don't want to be venting any of that smoke, etc. into the house. 

   Though, the nice thing about a really well designed and efficient rocket stove, is that once it gets up to temp and burning well, there is very little in the way of exhaust pollution.  I recently attended a workshop on cob rocket stove building, and the people who had built there's into the house, said that when it was up to temp, it burned extremely cleanly, and you could stick your face into the exhaust and it would mostly just be warm water vapor. I think they had also tested it with a CO monitor and it was very low.

  And on that note, I will be using a lot of insulation to achieve that efficiency.  For example, in between the two barrels will be rockwool or perlite. Wrapped around the outer barrel will be an IR reflector. The inner barrel will be lined (where the fire will be directly) with some insulated cob and then also a layer of NightHawkInLite's approx. Starlite material).  The pipe leading into the window will be insulated, etc.  Heck, even the vent/exhaust of the chimney, will be insulated some.

  I would prefer to use Solar heating, to be honest, but our property has so many trees and shade that we just don't get enough direct sun in the winter for this to be practical. We would have to cut down a few key, very large trees and we just can't afford that. Besides, it helps to keep our house cooler in the summer and our summers are worse than our winters (northern part of the south east coast of US, or more simply, the mid Atlantic region).

    I also plan to wrap the outside of the inner barrel with some copper tubing, and have that run to an outside hot tub. The inside, smaller barrel is 31 gallons (which I already have and use to burn yard debris) and the outside, larger barrel will be like a 55 gallon drum. 

  This project will be on hold until it starts to cool down more.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the stone acts as a thermal store - but just to ask have you actually made a heater yet? - first thing - make the heater - second thing see how it goes with heating a thermal store - like rocks or maybe sand? -thrd thing - a method to transfer that heat out - maybe a water heat exchanger? step by step mate keep it simple

replies.author: @simonsays5587
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I use a big flowerpot and tealights. Reduces the number of times the central heating turns on a lot. First I have to grow some seeds to make my own oil next year. ATM packing up. Will only have a wood stove, so this will absolutely be a project next year 👍🖐️

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text: Soapstone is some of the best thermal mass/storage. Water is very good too, but obviously you have to be careful with steam and pressure build up when using water.
